title: Postoperative Pain Management: Assessment and Multimodal Comfort
subtitle: A 55-year-old on the first day after abdominal surgery rates his incisional pain 8/10 and is splinting and reluctant to cough. The nurse assesses the pain, provides multimodal analgesia, and monitors for opioid side effects.

patient.name: Carl Whitman
patient.admitDate: Surgical unit, POD1 after open abdominal surgery (bowel resection). Reports 8/10 incisional pain with guarding, reluctant to cough or deep breathe. On scheduled non-opioid analgesia with opioid for breakthrough pain.

labs.r: Pain is what the patient says.

nursesNote: Carl Whitman, 55, POD1 open abdominal surgery, rating incisional pain 8/10 with guarding and reluctant to cough/deep breathe (atelectasis risk). Pain is subjective — the patient's self-report is the most reliable indicator; normal vital signs do not rule out pain. Provide multimodal analgesia (scheduled non-opioid + opioid for breakthrough, around-the-clock for constant pain) plus non-pharmacologic measures (positioning, splinting the incision, ice/heat, relaxation). Start a bowel regimen with opioids and monitor sedation level and respiratory rate (sedation precedes respiratory depression). Do not undertreat over addiction fears; reassess pain and function after each intervention and document.
providerOrders: Assess pain with self-report and a validated scale; set a comfort-function goal
Multimodal analgesia: scheduled non-opioid plus opioid for breakthrough pain
Non-pharmacologic comfort measures (positioning, incision splinting, ice/heat, relaxation)
Start a bowel regimen with opioids; monitor sedation level and respiratory rate
Reassess pain after each intervention and document the response

opts.text: The patient's self-report is the most reliable indicator of pain
Use a validated pain scale; behavioral tools for nonverbal patients
Unrelieved pain impairs coughing, deep breathing, and mobility
Multimodal analgesia combines non-opioids, opioids, and non-drug measures
Normal vital signs prove the patient is not in pain
Constant pain is best managed with around-the-clock dosing
Assess location, quality, intensity, timing, and effect on function
Combine non-opioids with opioids to improve relief and limit opioid dose
Opioids commonly cause constipation, so start a bowel regimen
Sedation precedes opioid-induced respiratory depression — monitor it
Fear of addiction justifies undertreating acute post-op pain
Non-pharmacologic measures complement medication
Assess the pain using the patient's self-report and a scale
Provide multimodal analgesia, around-the-clock for constant pain
Add non-pharmacologic comfort measures
Monitor side effects (sedation/RR) and start a bowel regimen
Reassess pain after the intervention and adjust; document
Accept the patient's self-report as the most reliable indicator
Use multimodal analgesia (non-opioid + opioid + non-drug measures)
Give around-the-clock analgesia for constant post-op pain
Start a bowel regimen to prevent opioid-induced constipation
Monitor sedation level and respiratory rate with opioids
Reassess pain after giving an analgesic
Withhold pain medication until the patient "proves" the pain
Assume normal vital signs mean there is no real pain
Wait until the pain is severe before treating it

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Believe and assess the patient's reported pain
Administer the ordered multimodal analgesia
Add positioning, incision splinting, and other comfort measures
Monitor sedation/respiratory status and bowel function
Reassess pain and function, then document the response
The pain score drops to his goal and he can cough and ambulate
Multimodal and non-drug measures keep pain controlled
A bowel regimen prevents constipation on opioids
He reports pain early and it is treated before it escalates
Pain stays severe because doses are delayed or withheld
Untreated pain prevents deep breathing, leading to atelectasis
Opioid-induced constipation develops with no bowel regimen
Oversedation with a drop in respiratory rate goes unrecognized

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
